intro:
Making extra payments on your mortgage is one of the most powerful financial moves a homeowner can make. Even a small monthly overpayment can save tens of thousands of dollars in interest and shave years off your loan term — often delivering returns far better than low-risk savings accounts.

Our mortgage overpayment calculator shows exactly how much you save in interest and how many months you shave off your loan by adding any extra amount to your monthly payment. It compares your standard amortization with the accelerated schedule side by side, making the benefit of overpaying immediately clear.

For a typical $300,000 mortgage at 6.5% over 30 years, an extra $200/month saves over $60,000 in interest and pays off the loan 6 years early. The earlier in the loan term you begin overpaying, the greater the impact — since more of each early payment goes toward interest.
howItWorks: Enter your original loan amount, annual interest rate, loan term in years, and the extra monthly amount you plan to pay. The calculator runs two amortization schedules — standard and with overpayment — then computes months saved, total interest saved, and compares the two interest totals.
interpretationGuide: Months saved shows how much sooner you will be debt-free. Interest saved is the total reduction in interest cost. The larger your overpayment as a percentage of the standard payment, the more dramatic the savings. Even $50/month extra compounds significantly over a 30-year term.

faqs.question: How much extra should I pay each month?
faqs.answer: Any extra amount helps. A rule of thumb: 1 extra monthly payment per year (divide monthly payment by 12 and add that amount monthly) typically saves 4–7 years on a 30-year mortgage. Even $100–$200 extra per month can save $30,000–$60,000 in interest.

faqs.question: Is there a penalty for overpaying a mortgage?
faqs.answer: In the US, most mortgages (especially conventional loans backed by Fannie Mae/Freddie Mac) have no prepayment penalty. Some loans, particularly adjustable-rate mortgages and portfolio loans, may have prepayment penalties in the first 3–5 years — check your loan documents.

faqs.question: Should I overpay my mortgage or invest instead?
faqs.answer: If your mortgage rate is above 5–6%, overpaying offers a guaranteed return equal to your rate. If your rate is 3–4%, investing in a diversified stock portfolio historically outperforms. Consider your risk tolerance, tax deductibility, and emotional value of being mortgage-free.

faqs.question: What is bi-weekly mortgage payment and how does it help?
faqs.answer: Paying half your monthly payment every two weeks results in 26 half-payments = 13 full monthly payments per year instead of 12. The extra annual payment reduces a 30-year mortgage by about 4–5 years and saves significant interest without feeling like a large extra outlay.

faqs.question: Does overpaying reduce my monthly payment or the loan term?
faqs.answer: With most mortgages, extra payments reduce the balance and shorten the term — your required monthly payment stays the same. Some lenders offer 're-amortization' (recasting) for a fee, which reduces the monthly payment while keeping the original term.

faqs.question: Can I overpay a fixed-rate mortgage at any time?
faqs.answer: Yes, on most standard fixed-rate mortgages. Simply add extra to your regular payment and specify it should go toward principal reduction. Sending a separate check or using an online payment portal labeled 'principal only' ensures it is applied correctly.

faqs.question: How do I make sure extra payments go to principal?
faqs.answer: Always specify that extra payments are for 'principal only' — do not pay ahead (which advances your due date, not reducing balance). Online banking systems usually have a principal payment option; if paying by check, write 'principal only' on the memo line.

faqs.question: What is mortgage recasting?
faqs.answer: Mortgage recasting (or re-amortization) lets you make a large lump sum payment, then re-amortize the remaining balance over the original remaining term, lowering your monthly payment. Most lenders charge a small fee ($150–$500) and require a minimum lump sum (often $5,000–$10,000).

faqs.question: Does overpaying help more early or late in the loan?
faqs.answer: Overpaying early in the loan term has the greatest impact because more of each payment goes toward interest when the balance is high. An extra $10,000 in year 1 of a 30-year mortgage saves far more than the same $10,000 in year 25.

faqs.question: How does overpaying affect my mortgage interest tax deduction?
faqs.answer: Overpaying reduces the loan balance faster, which means you accrue less interest — and therefore have a smaller mortgage interest deduction for itemized filers. However, the actual interest saved far exceeds the tax savings lost, especially since the standard deduction means many homeowners don't itemize anyway.
